Search results: 



--- SOURCE 1: Microscopic Techniques for Nanomaterials Characterization ---
URL: https://analyticalsciencejournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jemt.24799

SUMMARY:
Currently, scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), and atomic force microscope (AFM) are in trend to study the topography and tomography of nanomaterials (Merugu and Gothalwal 2021).



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Transmission Electron Microscopy | Nanoscience Instruments ---
URL: https://www.nanoscience.com/techniques/transmission-electron-microscopy/

SUMMARY:
To form a TEM image, a high energy electron beam is accelerated through an extremely thin “electron transparent” sample, typically thinner than 100 nm. Themicroscope columnconsists of a series of electromagnetic lenses and apertures to focus the electron beam onto the sample and magnify the TEM image onto the viewing screen (or detectors). Bragg diffraction by a crystalline material: incident radiation (e.g., the electron beam in the TEM) with a wavelength comparable to the interatomic spacings (d) is scattered, undergoing constructive interference at the characteristic angles known as Bragg angles (θB). Therefore, by placing the objective aperture around the direct beam (optic axis), these high-angle-scattered electrons are excluded from the image plane and hence a bright field (BF) image is formed.

FULL CONTENT:
Transmission Electron Microscopy | Nanoscience Instruments

enriched_points.expanded_content: Electron microscopy primarily includes Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), each offering unique imaging capabilities for nanomaterials characterization.
SEM provides 3D surface images with high resolution, while TEM offers detailed internal structural insights, allowing for atomic-scale observations of nanomaterials.
These techniques reveal crystal structures, particle size, morphology, and surface characteristics, essential for understanding the properties and applications of nanoporous materials.
Electron microscopy is widely used in fields like nanotechnology, materials science, and biomedical research, enabling advancements in drug delivery systems and nanomaterial design.
Challenges include sample preparation complexities, potential damage to sensitive materials, and the need for vacuum conditions, which may limit certain applications in real-world scenarios.
